import pygame
import random

# Define the screen dimensions
SCREEN_WIDTH = 1500
SCREEN_HEIGHT = 800

# Define the colors
BLACK = (0, 0, 0)

# Define the artists and their monthly Spotify listeners
artists = {
    "$NOT": {"Image":"./fotos/$NOT/$NOT_1.jpeg", "listeners": 7781046},

    "21 Savage": {"Image":"./fotos/21_Savage/21 Savage_1.jpeg", "listeners": 60358167},

    "9lokknine": {"Image":"./fotos/9lokknine/9lokknine_1.jpeg", "listeners": 1680245},

    "A Boogie Wit Da Hoodie": {"Image":"./fotos/A_Boogie_Wit_Da_Hoodie/A Boogie Wit Da Hoodie_1.jpeg", "listeners": 18379137},

    "Ayo & Teo": {"Image":"./fotos/Ayo_&_Teo/Ayo & Teo_1.jpeg", "listeners": 1818645},

    "Bhad Bhabie": {"Image":"./fotos/Bhad_Bhabie/Bhad Bhabie_1.jpeg", "listeners": 1915352},

    "Blueface": {"Image":"./fotos/Blueface/Blueface_1.jpeg", "listeners": 4890312},
}

# Initialize the game
pygame.init()

# Define the font and font size for the artist names
font = pygame.font.Font(None, 36)

# Initialize the font module
pygame.font.init()

# Initialize the game window and caption
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT))
pygame.display.set_caption("Guess the Artist")

# Create a clock object to control the frame rate
clock = pygame.time.Clock()

score = 0

while True:

    # Select two random artists
    first_artist, second_artist = random.sample(list(artists.keys()), 2)

    # Define the desired width and height for the images
    image_width = 800
    image_height = 600

    # Load the images for the artists
    first_artist_image = pygame.image.load(artists[first_artist]["Image"]).convert()
    second_artist_image = pygame.image.load(artists[second_artist]["Image"]).convert()

    # Resize the first artist's image
    first_artist_image = pygame.transform.scale(first_artist_image, (image_width, image_height))

    # Resize the second artist's image
    second_artist_image = pygame.transform.scale(second_artist_image, (image_width, image_height))

    # Display the images
    screen.blit(first_artist_image, (0, 0))
    screen.blit(second_artist_image, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, 0))

    # Render the text surfaces for the artist names
    first_artist_text = font.render(f"1 - {first_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255))
    second_artist_text = font.render(f"2 - {second_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255))

    # Blit the artist names
    screen.blit(first_artist_text, (0, image_height))
    screen.blit(second_artist_text, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, image_height))

    # Clear the screen
    screen.fill(BLACK)

   # Update the display
    pygame.display.flip()

    # Prompt the player for their guess
    guess = input(f"\nWhich artist has more monthly Spotify listeners - 1. {first_artist.title()} or 2. {second_artist.title()}? ")
    guess_lower = guess.strip().lower()

    if guess_lower == 'quit':
        print("Thanks for playing!")
        break

    elif guess_lower not in ['1', '2', first_artist.lower(), second_artist.lower()]:
        print("Invalid input. Please enter the name of one of the two artists or 'quit' to end the game.")

    elif guess_lower == first_artist.lower() or guess_lower == '1' and artists[first_artist]["listeners"] > artists[second_artist]["listeners"]:
        print(f"You guessed correctly! {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners'] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners'] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.")
        score += 1

    elif guess_lower == second_artist.lower() or guess_lower == '2' and artists[second_artist]["listeners"] > artists[first_artist]["listeners"]:
        print(f"You guessed correctly! {second_artist.title()} had {artists[second_artist]['listeners'] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {first_artist.title()} has {artists[first_artist]['listeners'] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.")
        score += 1

    else:
        print(f"\nSorry, you guessed incorrectly. {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners'] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners'] / 1e6:.1f}M monthly Spotify listeners.\n")
        print(f"Your current score is {score}.")

        # AskApologies for the incomplete response. Here's the continuation of the code:


        # Ask the player if they want to play again
        play_again = input("Would you like to play again? (y/n) ")
        if play_again.lower() == 'n':
            print(f"Your final score is {score}. Thanks for playing!")
            break
        else:
            score = 0

why arent the images and text shown

The issue could be that the code to display the images and text is placed after the screen has been cleared using `screen.fill(BLACK)`. This causes the images and text to be displayed on a black screen, making them invisible.

To fix this, you should move the lines that display the images and text above the `screen.fill(BLACK)` line. This way, the images and text will be displayed before the screen is cleared.

Here's the modified code:

```python
# ...

while True:
    # ...

    # Display the images
    screen.blit(first_artist_image, (0, 0))
    screen.blit(second_artist_image, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, 0))

    # Render the text surfaces for the artist names
    first_artist_text = font.render(f"1 - {first_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255))
    second_artist_text = font.render(f"2 - {second_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255))

    # Blit the artist names
    screen.blit(first_artist_text, (0, image_height))
    screen.blit(second_artist_text, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, image_height))

    # Update the display
    pygame.display.flip()

    # Clear the screen
    screen.fill(BLACK)

    # ...
```

By moving the display code above `screen.fill(BLACK)`, the images and text should now be visible on the screen.
You're welcome! I'm glad the suggestion worked for you. If you have any more questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask. Happy coding!

dont write code but is it possible to make the whole game happen in a pygame window and how

Yes, it is possible to create a game using Pygame that runs entirely within a Pygame window. Pygame provides a versatile framework for creating interactive games and applications. Here's a general outline of how you can make your game happen within a Pygame window:

1. Set up the Pygame window: Use `pygame.init()` to initialize the Pygame library, create a window with `pygame.display.set_mode()`, and set a caption for your game window with `pygame.display.set_caption()`.

2. Create game objects: Define the elements and entities within your game. This can include characters, objects, backgrounds, and any other visual or interactive elements.

3. Handle user input: Use Pygame's event handling system (`pygame.event.get()`) to capture user input, such as mouse clicks or keyboard presses. You can then respond to these inputs accordingly and update the state of your game objects.

4. Update game state: Implement the logic for your game by updating the positions, properties, and behaviors of your game objects. This typically involves updating the positions of characters, checking for collisions, managing game rules, and updating scores or player statistics.

5. Render the game: Each frame, redraw the game window to reflect the current state of your game objects. Use `pygame.Surface` objects to represent the graphics and draw them with Pygame's rendering functions like `pygame.draw()` and `pygame.blit()`.

6. Repeat the game loop: Run the game loop continuously to keep the game updating and rendering. This loop iterates through the steps of capturing user input, updating the game state, and rendering the game until the game is finished or the player decides to quit.

By following this approach, you can create a complete game that runs within a Pygame window. Remember to handle events, update the game state, and redraw the screen in each iteration of the game loop to keep the game running smoothly.

Additionally, Pygame provides various modules and functions to handle game features such as sound, animation, and collision detection, which you can incorporate into your game as needed.

I hope this information helps you in creating your game! Let me know if you have any further questions.
how can i make the names appear in the center of the image but below it

To make the artist names appear in the center of the image but below it, you can use the `get_rect()` method of the text surface to obtain its width and height. Then you can calculate the desired position for the text surface and use the `blit()` function to display it on the Pygame window. Here's an example of how you can modify the code:

1. Calculate the desired position for the text surface:
```python
artist_text_width = first_artist_text.get_rect().width
artist_text_height = first_artist_text.get_rect().height

artist_text_x = (image_width - artist_text_width) // 2
artist_text_y = image_height + (SCREEN_HEIGHT - image_height - artist_text_height) // 2
```

2. Display the text surfaces using the calculated positions:
```python
screen.blit(first_artist_text, (artist_text_x, artist_text_y))
screen.blit(second_artist_text, (artist_text_x + SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, artist_text_y))
```

By calculating the appropriate position using the width and height of the text surface, the artist names will be centered below the image.

Here's the modified section of code:

```python
# ...

# Render the text surfaces for the artist names
first_artist_text = font.render(f"1 - {first_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255))
second_artist_text = font.render(f"2 - {second_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255))

# Calculate the desired position for the text surfaces
artist_text_width = first_artist_text.get_rect().width
artist_text_height = first_artist_text.get_rect().height

artist_text_x = (image_width - artist_text_width) // 2
artist_text_y = image_height + (SCREEN_HEIGHT - image_height - artist_text_height) // 2

# Blit the artist names
screen.blit(first_artist_text, (artist_text_x, artist_text_y))
screen.blit(second_artist_text, (artist_text_x + SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, artist_text_y))

# ...
```

By making these modifications, the artist names should appear centered below the respective images in your Pygame window.

while True:

    # Select two random artists

    first_artist_image = pygame.image.load(artists[first_artist]["Image"]).convert()
    second_artist_image = pygame.image.load(artists[second_artist]["Image"]).convert()

    # Resize the first artist's image

    screen.blit(second_artist_image, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, 0))

    # Render the text surfaces for the artist names

    artist_text_height = first_artist_text.get_rect().height

    artist_text_x = (image_width - artist_text_width) // 2
    artist_text_y = image_height + (SCREEN_HEIGHT - image_height - artist_text_height) // 2

    # Blit the artist names

    screen.blit(second_artist_text, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, image_height))

    # Update the display
    pygame.display.flip()

    # Clear the screen
    screen.fill(BLACK)

like this?

Almost! There is a small change you need to make in the code. Instead of the `artist_text_y` variable, you should use `artist_text_y + image_height` in the `screen.blit()` function to position the artist names below the images. Here's the modified section of the code:

```python
# ...

# Blit the artist names
screen.blit(first_artist_text, (artist_text_x, artist_text_y + image_height))
screen.blit(second_artist_text, (artist_text_x + SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, artist_text_y + image_height))

# ...
```

By adding `image_height` to the y-coordinate in `screen.blit()`, you properly position the artist names below the respective images.

Here's the updated code:

```python
# ...

# Blit the artist names
screen.blit(first_artist_text, (artist_text_x, artist_text_y + image_height))
screen.blit(second_artist_text, (artist_text_x + SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, artist_text_y + image_height))

# ...
```

With this modification, the artist names should now appear centered below the respective images in your Pygame window.

Apologies for the confusion. It seems that the issue lies in the order of the code lines. You need to display the images and text before you clear the screen with `screen.fill(BLACK)`. Here's the correct order of operations:

```python
# ...

# Display the images
screen.blit(first_artist_image, (0, 0))
screen.blit(second_artist_image, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, 0))

# Calculate the desired position for the text surfaces
artist_text_width = first_artist_text.get_rect().width
artist_text_height = first_artist_text.get_rect().height

artist_text_x = (image_width - artist_text_width) // 2
artist_text_y = image_height + (SCREEN_HEIGHT - image_height - artist_text_height) // 2

# Blit the artist names
screen.blit(first_artist_text, (artist_text_x, artist_text_y + image_height))
screen.blit(second_artist_text, (artist_text_x + SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, artist_text_y + image_height))

# Update the display
pygame.display.flip()

# Clear the screen
screen.fill(BLACK)

# ...
```

By reordering the code in this way, the images will be displayed, followed by the artist names centered below them. Then, the screen will be cleared, and the updated display will be shown.

I apologize for the confusion caused by the previous responses. This reordered code should now display the images and text correctly in your game.
